variant_description: Piece Picking variant: one picker picks items for one order at a time. Simplest baseline model — the picker's route is the visiting sequence of the single order's items, often optimized with TSP heuristics. Serves Scenario 1 (Picking Strategy Selection) as the reference case against which batch/zone/wave/goods-to-person strategies are compared. Target technology not yet committed — natural candidates include simpy for teaching or anylogic/simio for production models.

Batch Picking variant: multiple orders consolidated into one pick route to reduce total walking distance. Configurable batch size parameter creates a trade-off between efficiency (larger batches) and responsiveness (smaller batches). Serves Scenarios 1 and 3 (Picking Strategy Selection and Batch Size Optimization).

Zone Picking variant: warehouse divided into zones, each with its own picker pool; cross-zone order consolidation happens downstream. Parallel zone-level streams merge at a consolidation point before packing. Serves Scenarios 1 and 2 (Picking Strategy Selection and Picker Staffing / Zone Allocation).

Wave Picking variant: hybrid of batch and zone approaches. Waves of orders pass through zones sequentially, with zone-level picking aggregated between zones. This is the strategy DHL used in the AnyLogic case study (context row 5) — hence des_product_code is 'anylogic'. Serves Scenarios 1, 2, and 4 (strategy selection, staffing, automation decision baseline).

Goods-to-Person (automated sortation) variant: picker is stationary at an ergonomic station; automated retrieval systems (conveyors, AS/RS, shuttles, mobile robots) bring items to the picker. Eliminates walking entirely. Serves Scenario 4 (Automation Investment Decision) as the 'automated' alternative in a comparative analysis against manual picking variants.
